text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE VISIT — 24-MONTH POST-TREATMENT COMPREHENSIVE ASSESSMENT

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo, a 54-year-old Nigerian-American woman and never-smoker, presents for her 24-month post-chemoradiation comprehensive surveillance visit for HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, treated definitively with concurrent IMRT and cis-based chemotherapy approximately 2 years ago. She has no new symptoms or complaints today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo's oncologic history is as follows: She was initially diagnosed two years ago at age 52 with a 2.2 cm HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with ipsilateral level IIA nodal involvement (a single 2.7 cm necrotic left level IIA node), staged as T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition HPV-oropharynx classification. She underwent PET-CT staging confirming locoregional disease only, with no distant metastases identified. She had NGS performed on the primary specimen which showed a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation and several other co-occurring alterations (TRAF3 loss-of-function, EP300, FBXW7, KMT2D mutations); TMB was low at 4 mut/Mb and MSS; PD-L1 CPS was 18. No actionable fusions were identified.

She was treated with definitive IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost technique (70 Gy / 63 Gy / 56 Gy in 35 fractions to high-, intermediate-, and low-risk planning target volumes respectively) with concurrent Platinol 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22 (full dose) and a dose-reduced cycle on day 43 (75 mg/m2) due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 3 mucositis. She required inpatient admission during week 4 of treatment for grade 3 confluent oropharyngeal mucositis, IV morphine PCA, and IV hydration, with full enteral nutritional support via prophylactically placed PEG tube. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis, grade 2 nephrotoxicity with Platinol-related magnesium wasting, and grade 1-2 xerostomia evolving during treatment. She completed all 35 radiation fractions without interruption.

Post-treatment PET-CT at 10 weeks showed a complete metabolic response at both the primary site and the involved lymph node. The PEG tube was removed at approximately 3 months post-treatment after she achieved full oral intake. Surveillance imaging at 6 months and 12 months (CT neck/chest with contrast) showed no evidence of recurrence, with the previously involved left level IIA node reducing to an 8 mm fibrotic remnant. At 18 months, a surveillance CT neck was again negative. She has had no episodes of clinical concern for recurrence throughout the follow-up period.

She was noted at 8 months post-treatment to have grade 2 radiation fibrosis of the left neck (cervical stiffness, soft tissue induration, mild limited range of motion), treated with physical therapy, and radiation-induced hypothyroidism with TSH rising to 7.2, for which her levothyroxine dose was adjusted from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Her TSH subsequently normalized.

Today at the 2-year mark, she reports doing very well. She describes her swallowing as near-normal — she is eating a full diet with only very minor modifications (avoids extremely hard or dry foods such as raw carrots and saltine crackers, which cause mild discomfort). She says this is not significantly impacting her daily quality of life. Xerostomia is present but mild; she uses Biotene mouth spray and xylitol lozenges, which she finds helpful. She has been back to full-time work as a high school English teacher, which she describes as very meaningful and she is thriving professionally. She denies any new throat discomfort, odynophagia, referred otalgia, sensation of a neck mass, unexplained weight loss, hemoptysis, or any new constitutional symptoms. She has not experienced significant fatigue. She is sleeping well and engaged in regular light exercise (walking and yoga). She remains a never-smoker with no alcohol use.

She expresses gratitude to the care team and notes that she has connected with an HPV-positive head and neck cancer survivor support group, which she finds beneficial from a psychosocial standpoint. She has questions today about long-term surveillance planning, dental health, and what the "2-year mark" means prognostically.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No unintentional weight loss. Good energy overall.
- Head/neck/ENT: Mild, stable xerostomia managed with salivary substitutes. Mild dysphagia to very hard, dry foods only — not progressive. No throat pain, odynophagia, referred otalgia, hoarseness, or voice change. No epistaxis or hemoptysis. No new neck swelling or palpable masses. Left neck stiffness improved since physical therapy.
- Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- GI: Appetite intact. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. PEG tube site fully healed; no issues.
- Endocrine: On adjusted levothyroxine dose with TSH normalized as of last check.
- Musculoskeletal: Left neck range of motion improved with physical therapy. No new joint complaints.
- Neurologic: No peripheral neuropathy, tinnitus, or hearing complaints (baseline audiogram performed prior to cis was normal; repeat during treatment showed no significant ototoxicity).
- Psychiatric/psychosocial: Coping well. Engaged with survivor support group. No clinically significant anxiety or depression reported.
- Dermatologic: Left neck radiation dermatitis fully resolved; some residual skin texture changes in the left neck radiation field, not bothersome.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversational. Mood and affect appropriate and upbeat.

Vital Signs: Weight 68.5 kg (BMI 24.3), blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 99% on room air.

Head and Neck:
- Inspection: No visible masses or asymmetry of the neck. Left neck skin shows mild residual post-radiation hyperpigmentation and subtle subcutaneous tissue texture changes consistent with post-radiation changes; no active dermatitis. No ulceration.
- Palpation: No palpable lymphadenopathy in bilateral cervical chains (levels I through V), submandibular, submental, posterior cervical, or supraclavicular regions. Left level II region is palpably soft and fibrotic in quality, without a discrete nodal mass — consistent with known post-treatment changes. No tenderness. No tracheal deviation. Thyroid: not enlarged; no nodules appreciated.
- Cervical Range of Motion: Near-full range of motion bilaterally; very mild residual limitation on left lateral rotation, improved from prior visits. No pain with neck movement at today's visit.
- Oral Cavity: Dentition in fair condition; patient is compliant with fluoride treatments per dental oncology. Mild salivary pooling reduction consistent with xerostomia. No mucosal ulceration, leukoplakia, or erythema suspicious for malignancy in the oral cavity or visible oropharynx.

Flexible Fiberoptic Laryngoscopy (performed in clinic by head and neck oncology attending):
- Base of tongue: Mucosa appears smooth and intact. There is mild post-radiation erythema and subtle mucosal irregularity consistent with treated field and submucosal fibrosis, but no discrete mass, ulceration, or necrotic-appearing tissue. No submucosal fullness suspicious for deep-seated tumor. The treated left base of tongue area is healed and the midline tongue base mucosa is symmetric.
- Vallecula: Patent bilaterally, no mass effect.
- Epiglottis: Normal appearance.
- Vocal folds: Symmetric, fully mobile bilaterally. No lesions. Voice normal quality.
- Posterior pharyngeal wall: No suspicious lesions.
- Nasopharynx: No mass or adenoid tissue abnormality.
- Overall laryngoscopic impression: No evidence of gross mucosal recurrence.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site fully healed; no skin changes or tenderness at the former ostomy site.
Extremities: No edema, clubbing, or lymphedema.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II through XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today)
- CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^9/L (differential within normal limits), hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, platelets 218 x10^9/L. No cytopenias.
- Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L. BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2, back to pre-treatment baseline). Glucose 92 mg/dL. AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 64 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL. Albumin 4.1 g/dL. All within normal limits.
- TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine 100 mcg daily). Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal).
- Magnesium: 2.1 mg/dL (normal; previously had Platinol-related magnesium wasting during treatment, now fully resolved).
- No additional labs ordered today beyond surveillance panel.

---

IMAGING RESULTS
No new imaging performed at today's visit. Most recent surveillance imaging was CT of the neck with contrast at 18 months post-treatment, which showed no evidence of locoregional recurrence, stable fibrotic left level IIA nodal remnant, and no new lymphadenopathy. No chest imaging was performed at the 18-month time point per the team's evolving surveillance protocol based on the favorable trajectory. The treating team is reviewing the timing of next imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition Stage I), two years post-definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy (IMRT + Platinol).
   - Comprehensive clinical assessment today is entirely reassuring. Flexible laryngoscopy shows no evidence of mucosal recurrence. There is no palpable adenopathy. The patient is asymptomatic from an oncologic standpoint.
   - She has achieved a durable complete response now confirmed at 2 years. The 2-year milestone is clinically meaningful in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer: while recurrences can occur beyond this point (and late relapses at 3-5 years have been described in the literature, particularly in smokers), for a never-smoking patient with HPV-positive disease and complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET, the risk of recurrence at this point is substantially reduced. I counseled her that current estimates suggest her 5-year disease-specific survival exceeds 85-90% based on contemporary series for her risk profile, and the probability of cure is high. She received and processed this information very well.
   - Given the 2-year milestone and stable course, I discussed transitioning her surveillance interval. Per our institutional head and neck oncology surveillance protocol — and consistent with the NCCN guidelines for oropharyngeal SCC in the post-treatment setting — we will continue clinical visits every 6 months through year 3, then annually through year 5, with clinical examination and flexible laryngoscopy at each visit.
   - For imaging: I discussed the evolving evidence around surveillance CT imaging in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer patients who have achieved complete metabolic response. There is no level I evidence demonstrating a survival benefit from routine cross-sectional imaging beyond 12-18 months in asymptomatic patients with complete remission. We will obtain a CT of the neck and chest with contrast at the 30-month mark (approximately 6 months from now) as a scheduled interval imaging study. If this remains negative, we will reassess whether further routine imaging is indicated versus symptom-driven imaging only in years 3-5. PET-CT is not routinely indicated at this interval in the absence of clinical concern.
   - The patient was counseled to return promptly for any new symptoms: new neck mass, persistent throat pain, odynophagia, referred otalgia, unexplained weight loss, hoarseness, or any palpable change in the neck.

2. Late effects of chemoradiation — Xerostomia.
   - Grade 1 xerostomia, stable and manageable. Patient using Biotene spray and xylitol-containing lozenges with good effect. Pilocarpine was discussed but the patient does not feel the burden is significant enough to warrant systemic therapy at this time. She will continue current measures. I encouraged her to maintain excellent oral hydration.
   - Salivary function is unlikely to recover further at this stage post-radiation; I counseled her on the plateau of salivary gland recovery typically seen by 18-24 months post-IMRT with modern parotid-sparing techniques. Mean parotid doses during treatment were favorable (left 24 Gy, right 22 Gy), which likely contributed to her relatively mild xerostomia.

3. Late effects of chemoradiation — Dysphagia.
   - Residual mild dysphagia to hard/dry foods, not progressive, not limiting diet significantly. She had a modified barium swallow at 12 months showing mild pharyngeal dysmotility with reduced tongue base retraction, no aspiration. She is managing well with dietary modifications.
   - She completed formal speech-language pathology therapy and has maintained exercises independently. No need for formal re-referral at this time given her clinical trajectory, but she knows to reach out if dysphagia worsens.
   - I discussed the risk of progressive late-onset dysphagia (Lund dysphagia syndrome) as a known long-term sequela of oropharyngeal radiation, and emphasized the importance of maintaining swallowing exercises and oral intake to prevent progressive disuse atrophy of pharyngeal musculature.

4. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — stable.
   - TSH 1.9 on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. Free T4 normal. Continue current dose. Annual TSH monitoring.

5. Late effects of chemoradiation — Radiation fibrosis, left neck.
   - Previously grade 2 per LENT-SOMA criteria, improved with physical therapy. Clinical exam today shows near-full range of motion with only very mild residual limitation. The patient has been doing home cervical exercises independently. She does not feel this is significantly impacting her quality of life. No referral needed at this time; continue home exercise program.

6. Dental surveillance.
   - Patient is compliant with dental oncology follow-up for radiation caries prevention (fluoride trays, regular dental cleanings). Continued compliance emphasized. Any tooth extractions in the future must be managed carefully in consultation with our dental oncology team given the risk of osteoradionecrosis of the mandible in the radiation field. She is counseled to inform all dental providers of her radiation treatment history.

7. Renal function.
   - Creatinine back to baseline 0.8 mg/dL. eGFR >60 mL/min. No ongoing nephrotoxicity from cis. Continue monitoring annually as part of routine CMP.

8. Survivorship and psychosocial support.
   - The patient is doing extremely well psychosocially. She has reconnected with her professional role fully and finds purpose and meaning in her work. She has engaged with an HPV-positive head and neck cancer survivor support group, which I strongly encouraged her to continue, as peer support has been shown to improve quality of life and facilitate coping in this population.
   - I provided a formal referral to our head and neck cancer survivorship program for a comprehensive survivorship care plan, including documentation of treatment summary, late effects monitoring recommendations, and primary care coordination.
   - Counseled on sun protection to the radiated left neck skin field, and on avoidance of tobacco and alcohol given their risk for second primary malignancy in the head and neck region (reinforced her never-smoker status).
   - HPV vaccination: Patient was treated for HPV-associated malignancy and is beyond the standard vaccination age range; vaccination is not indicated at this time per current guidelines.

9. ECOG Performance Status: 0. The patient has fully recovered functional status.

Return to clinic in 6 months for the 30-month surveillance visit. CT neck and chest with contrast to be scheduled at the 30-month interval. Labs (CBC, CMP, TSH) at next visit. Flexible laryngoscopy to be performed at each clinic visit. The patient verbalized understanding of the plan, had all questions answered, and expressed confidence in the follow-up schedule. She was reminded of our head and neck oncology nurse navigator contact for any interim concerns.

---

Time spent: 45 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care).

Electronic attestation and signature: [Head and Neck Oncology / Medical Oncology Attending]
